From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British trade unionist

For the convicted murderer, see [Dexter (TV series) § Andrew Conley](/wiki/Dexter_(TV_series)#Andrew_Conley "Dexter (TV series)").

**Andrew Conley** (18 December 1881[[1]](#cite_note-1) – 5 June 1952) was a [British](/wiki/British_people "British people") [trade unionist](/wiki/Trade_unionist "Trade unionist").

Born in [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds") to [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") parents,[[2]](#cite_note-labour-2) Conley fought in the [Second Boer War](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War").[[3]](#cite_note-kershen-3) He then worked as a garment maker,[[2]](#cite_note-labour-2) and became a branch secretary in the [Amalgamated Union of Clothiers Operatives](/wiki/Amalgamated_Union_of_Clothiers_Operatives "Amalgamated Union of Clothiers Operatives"), then national organiser of its successor, the [United Garment Workers' Trade Union](/wiki/United_Garment_Workers%27_Trade_Union "United Garment Workers' Trade Union").[[3]](#cite_note-kershen-3)

In 1920, various tailors' trade unions merged to form the [National Union of Tailors and Garment Workers](/wiki/National_Union_of_Tailors_and_Garment_Workers "National Union of Tailors and Garment Workers") (NUTGW). Although [Joseph Young](/wiki/Joseph_Young_(trade_unionist) "Joseph Young (trade unionist)") was seen as the obvious choice for its leadership, his health was failing, and he instead supported Conley's successful campaign for the general secretaryship.[[3]](#cite_note-kershen-3) In post, he focussed on absorbing other unions, arranging mergers with more than 20 before he retired in 1948. He was elected to the General Council of the [Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress") in 1921, and was its [President](/wiki/President_of_the_Trades_Union_Congress "President of the Trades Union Congress") in 1934, where he led centenary commemorations of the [Tolpuddle Martyrs](/wiki/Tolpuddle_Martyrs "Tolpuddle Martyrs").[[2]](#cite_note-labour-2)

Conley also supported women's trade unionism, encouraging women tailors to become active in the NUTGW,[[2]](#cite_note-labour-2) and he was succeeded as general secretary by [Anne Loughlin](/wiki/Anne_Loughlin "Anne Loughlin").
